request.question: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?

title: Catfish Creek (Iowa)
Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River)
Rainwater Creek
Passage Creek
North Fork Flathead River
Black Creek (Florida)
Smith Creek (Virginia)
Shenandoah River
Los Baños Creek
Yager Creek
sentences: Catfish Creek is a 21.2 mi tributary of the upper Mississippi River in northeastern Iowa.
It rises southwest of Dubuque, just southeast of Peosta.
The Middle Fork rises west of Dubuque and flows under and then parallel to U.S. Route 20; the former Illinois Central Railroad, now the Chicago Central and Pacific Railroad has its mainline in it.
The remaining forks of the creek are all south of U.S. Route 52.
Swiss Valley Park, maintained by Dubuque County, is along the main fork of the creek, which is a designated trout stream through the park.
In recent years, people have been trying to stop the erosion in this relatively flat way out of the Gorge of the Upper Mississippi.
The North Fork of the creek has been proposed for a trail/park.
Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.
It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.
Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.
Rainwater Creek is a 3.9 mi tributary stream of Lake Fork Creek.
It flows into Lake Fork Creek downstream from the Lake Fork Reservoir Dam approximately four miles west of Quitman in western Wood County, Texas.
Its primary source is a spring, augmented by stormwater runoff.
It is named for the family of William Walton Rainwater, who was among the earliest permanent settlers of the area.
Passage Creek is a 38.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in Fort Valley, Virginia.

For most of its length it flows through a rural valley between the two spine-like ridges of Massanutten Mountain, then exits the valley by cutting a narrow gorge through the northeast end of the mountain.
The North Fork Flathead River (Ktunaxa: "kqaskanmituk" ) is a 153 mi river flowing through British Columbia, Canada, south into the U.S. state of Montana.
It is one of the three primary forks of the Flathead River, the main inflow of Flathead Lake and a tributary of the Columbia River via the Clark Fork River and the Pend Oreille River.
The river is sometimes considered the upper headwaters of the Flathead River, although the North Fork is its official name in the U.S.
Other naming conventions for the river include Flathead River - North Fork, North Fork of Flathead River, and North Fork of the Flathead River.
Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.
It is located in Clay County, Florida.
Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.
North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.
North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.
South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.
The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.
Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.
Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.
Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.
The Shenandoah River is a tributary of the Potomac River, 55.6 mi long with two forks approximately 100 mi long each, in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.

The principal tributary of the Potomac, the river and its tributaries drain the central and lower Shenandoah Valley and the Page Valley in the Appalachians on the west side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, in northwestern Virginia and the Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia.
Los Baños Creek or Los Banos Creek, originally El Arroyo de los Baños, is a tributary stream of the San Joaquin River.
Its source drains the slopes of the Diablo Range within the Central Valley of California, United States.
Los Baños Creek has its source at the confluence of North Fork Los Banos Creek and South Fork Los Banos Creek.
It flows northeast to the west edge of Los Banos and then north to Mud Slough 2.5 miles (4 km) upstream from its confluence with the San Joaquin River.
Yager Creek is a tributary stream of the Van Duzen River on the north coast of California in Humboldt County, California.
It has its source at the confluence of North Fork Yager Creek and Middle Fork Yager Creek.
Its mouth is at the confluence with the Van Duzen River just below the town of Carlotta.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?
hop1_docs: Smith Creek (Virginia) | Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.  Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.  Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.

Hayfork Creek | Hayfork Creek is a tributary of the South Fork Trinity River in Northern California in the United States.  At over 50 mi long, it is the river's longest tributary and is one of the southernmost streams in the Klamath Basin.  It winds through a generally steep and narrow course north then west through the forested Klamath Mountains, but also passes through the Hayfork and Hyampom Valleys, which comprise the primary agricultural regions of Trinity County.

North Fork Embarras River | The North Fork Embarras River is a 64.0 mi stream which originates in Edgar County, Illinois, and flows through Clark, Crawford and Jasper counties.  Its confluence with the Embarras River is located near the town of Ste. Marie in Jasper County.  It is a major tributary to the Embarras River.  The North Fork's main tributary is Panther Creek, located in Jasper County.

Black Creek (Florida) | Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.  It is located in Clay County, Florida.  Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.  North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.  North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.  South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.  The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.

North Fork Smith River (California) | The North Fork Smith River is 28 mi tributary of the Smith River that begins in the U.S. state of Oregon and ends in the U.S. state of California.  Arising near Chetco Peak in the Klamath Mountains, it flows generally south to meet the Middle Fork Smith River at Gasquet, California.  The combined streams form the Smith River.

Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River) | Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.  It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.  Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.

South Fork Crooked River | The South Fork Crooked River is a tributary, 76 mi long, of the Crooked River in the U.S. state of Oregon.  Starting southeast of Hampton in Deschutes County, the South Fork flows east from near Ram Lake (dry).  It passes under U.S. Route 20 at Hampton, then continues east and north for about 6 mi before entering Crook County.  Shortly thereafter, east of Hampton Buttes, it receives Buck Creek from the right and then passes through Logan Reservoir.  Flowing north, it receives Sand Hollow Creek from the left, then Twelvemile Creek from the right before crossing under Oregon Route 380 (Southeast Paulina Highway).  Just north of the highway, the river joins Beaver Creek to form the Crooked River 125 mi from its confluence with the Deschutes River.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Smith Creek: passage [1] ("Smith Creek (Virginia)") states Smith Creek is a "35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River" and locates it in the "Shenandoah Valley of Virginia" (quotes abridged from passage [1]).
  - North Fork (supporting): passage [6] ("Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River)") mentions the "North Fork Shenandoah River" (supports river identification).
- Ambiguities:
  - Only one Smith Creek candidate appears in the passages: "Smith Creek (Virginia)". 
  - "North Fork" could generically refer to many "North Fork" rivers (e.g., North Fork Smith River appears in passage [5]) but the Smith Creek passage explicitly names the "North Fork of the Shenandoah River"; no alternative Shenandoah-related page is present in the provided set.
  - The expected page that lists the states traversed (Shenandoah River / North Fork Shenandoah River full article) is not included among the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as phrased in the passages): "the North Fork of the Shenandoah River".
  - Missing fact needed: the precise list of US states that the Shenandoah River (or its North Fork) passes through.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Shenandoah River" page — check the lede/infobox and the "Course" or "Geography" sections for a list of US states the river passes through. Search keywords: "Shenandoah River states", "Shenandoah River course".
  2) Open "North Fork Shenandoah River" page (if separate) — check lede/infobox and "Course" for state(s) covered. Search keywords: "North Fork Shenandoah River states", "North Fork Shenandoah course".
  3) Re-open "Smith Creek (Virginia)" lede/infobox to confirm the link wording to the North Fork Shenandoah River and ensure no local/state-only qualifiers appear.
  4) Optionally check "Shenandoah Valley" or categories like "Rivers of Virginia" / "Rivers of West Virginia" for corroboration.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: North River (South Fork Shenandoah River) | The North River is a 55.3 mi river in the mountains and Shenandoah Valley of northern Virginia, the United States.  It joins the South River at Port Republic to form the South Fork Shenandoah River.

Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River) | Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.  It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.  Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.
Shenandoah River Raymond R. "Andy" Guest Jr. State Park | Shenandoah River Raymond R. "Andy" Guest Jr.  State Park

Shenandoah River | The Shenandoah River is a tributary of the Potomac River, 55.6 mi long with two forks approximately 100 mi long each, in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.  The principal tributary of the Potomac, the river and its tributaries drain the central and lower Shenandoah Valley and the Page Valley in the Appalachians on the west side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, in northwestern Virginia and the Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia.

Cold Spring River | The Cold Spring River is a 3.9 mi mountain stream in Rockingham County in the U.S. state of Virginia.  It is a tributary of the German River, the principal source of the North Fork Shenandoah River.  Via the Shenandoah River, the Cold Spring River is part of the Potomac River watershed.

USS Shenandoah | Four United States Navy ships, including one rigid airship, and one ship of the Confederate States of America, have been named Shenandoah, after the Shenandoah River of western Virginia and West Virginia.

Shenandoah Retreat, Virginia | Shenandoah Retreat is an unincorporated community and census-designated place in eastern Clarke County, Virginia, on the east shore of the Shenandoah River.  The population as of the 2010 United States Census was 518.
hop2_titles: Shenandoah River Raymond R. "Andy" Guest Jr. State Park
response.summary_2: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River. The Shenandoah River is in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.
